channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: 23 Ways ChatGPT Still Sucks After 3 years (And How to Fix Them)
executive_summary: Despite three years of development, chatbots still struggle with collaboration, intent capture, and output formatting, making them less effective for users. Current tools lack features like threading and dynamic input options, leading to inefficient workflows and lost outputs. Improvements in user interface and proactive agents are essential for transforming chatbots into productive workbenches.
bullets: Current chatbots trap conversations between one user and the AI, lacking threading and branching tools, which leads to 'copy-paste ping pong' instead of real-time collaboration. This is like trying to build a Lego set alone while your friends are all building their own sets separately, missing out on the fun of creating together.

Users must prompt precisely for good results, as there’s no dynamic user interface to clarify inputs with options like checkboxes or sliders for personality and agenticness. It's like trying to order a custom sandwich without being able to see the ingredients; you might end up with something you didn’t want.

Exporting AI outputs is hampered by poor formatting, disappearing action items, and messy stakeholder sharing, which prevents smooth conversion into shippable work. Imagine trying to send a beautifully crafted cake to a party, but it arrives as a jumbled mess because it wasn’t packed properly.

Users cannot auto-refresh outputs, see diffs, or have agents that update themselves based on changing sources, requiring constant babysitting. It’s like having a pet that needs constant attention; if you don’t check on it regularly, it can get into trouble.

Citation handling, memory editing, privacy options, and cost visibility remain underdeveloped, limiting users' confidence in sensitive or high-stakes work. This is akin to walking a tightrope without a safety net; one misstep could lead to a disaster.

Finished work often gets buried without smart grouping, pinning, or deep in-chat search, forcing users to reinvent previous outputs. It’s like losing your keys in a messy room; you waste time searching instead of getting to where you need to go.

Version history, tone control, and dynamic form generation are missing, leading to wasted edits, tone whiplash, and repetitive data entry. Think of it like trying to write a novel without being able to track changes; every time you edit, you risk losing your original ideas.

Nate B Jones states, 'We’re three years into the LLM revolution, and it still shouldn’t suck this much to use a chatbot.' This highlights the frustration users feel when tools don’t meet their expectations after years of development.

The lack of granular privacy controls means users can't effectively manage who sees what, which is critical for sensitive projects. It's like having a diary with no lock; anyone can read your private thoughts.

The inability to dynamically adjust agenticness means users can't tailor the AI's personality or behavior on the fly, which limits personalization. Imagine trying to have a conversation with someone who can only speak in one tone; it quickly becomes dull and unengaging.

The current state of chatbots leads to a lack of trust in their outputs, especially for high-stakes work. This is similar to relying on a weather app that frequently gets the forecast wrong; you wouldn’t trust it to plan your outdoor event.

The need for smarter user interfaces and better export pipelines is critical to move chat from clever text exchanges to a true productivity workbench. Think of it like upgrading from a basic toolbox to a fully equipped workshop; the right tools make all the difference.
key_takeaways: Implement features that allow for real-time collaboration in chatbots, such as threading and branching. This is crucial because without these features, users will continue to face inefficiencies and frustration. If you skip this, your team will struggle to work together effectively.

Develop a dynamic user interface that includes options like checkboxes and sliders for intent capture. This matters because precise prompting is currently a barrier to getting good results. Without this, users will continue to receive outputs that don’t meet their needs.

Enhance export formatting to ensure clean and organized outputs. This is important because messy exports can lead to wasted time and effort in preparing work for stakeholders. If you don’t address this, your team may miss deadlines due to disorganization.

Create proactive agents that can auto-refresh outputs and adapt to changing sources. This is essential to reduce the constant need for manual updates. If you ignore this, you risk falling behind as information evolves.

Establish robust privacy controls and citation handling to build trust in the outputs. This is vital for users working on sensitive projects. Without it, you may lose clients or stakeholders who are concerned about data security.

By the end of the year, aim to incorporate version history and tone control features into chatbots. This is necessary to improve user experience and reduce wasted edits. If you don’t prioritize this, users will continue to feel frustrated and disengaged.

technical_terms.threading: A feature that allows multiple conversation paths within a chat, enabling users to discuss different topics simultaneously. Think of it like a tree with branches; each branch represents a different conversation thread.
technical_terms.branching: The ability to create separate paths in a conversation based on user inputs or decisions, allowing for more complex interactions. Like a choose-your-own-adventure book, where each choice leads to a different storyline.
technical_terms.agenticness: The level of autonomy or personality an AI can exhibit in its responses, allowing for tailored interactions. Think of it like a character in a video game that can change its behavior based on player choices.
technical_terms.export formatting: The way information is organized and presented when it is exported from a chatbot, impacting its usability. Like wrapping a gift; how you package it can affect how well it is received.
technical_terms.proactive agents: AI systems that can automatically update and adapt based on new information or changes in context. It’s like having a personal assistant who anticipates your needs before you even ask.
technical_terms.citation handling: The process of managing and displaying sources for information provided by the AI, which is crucial for credibility. Think of it like footnotes in a research paper; they give credit and context to the information presented.
technical_terms.memory editing: The ability to modify or delete information stored by the AI, which impacts how it remembers past interactions. Like editing a photo; you can remove unwanted elements to improve the overall picture.
technical_terms.privacy controls: Settings that allow users to manage who can see their data and outputs, essential for protecting sensitive information. It’s like having a lock on your front door; it keeps unwanted visitors out.
technical_terms.version history: A record of changes made to a document or output, allowing users to track edits over time. Think of it like a time machine for your work; you can go back to see previous versions.
technical_terms.tone control: The ability to adjust the emotional tone of the AI's responses, ensuring they match the desired communication style. Like adjusting the volume on a speaker; you can make it louder or softer to fit the situation.
technical_terms.dynamic forms: Forms that can change based on user input, making data entry more efficient and tailored. It’s like a chameleon that adapts to its environment; it changes to fit the needs of the user.
